coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect Au(III) and glutathione
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Jiangjiang
Hu, Donghua
Wang, Weina
Zhang, Qiuhong
Meng, Zhen
Jia, Xudong
Xi, Kai
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we reported for the first time that Au(III) decorated carbon dot cluster (Au(III)/CDC) was synthesized to detect glutathione through fluorescence “off–on” approach. The “off” process was realized by the introduction of Au(III) on luminescent carbon dots (CDs), which formed the complex of Au(III)/CDC and quenched the fluorescence of CDs efficiently. This “off” process was used to detect Au(III) with the selectivity among 21 metal ions and the limitation was 0.48μM (S/N=3). Au(III) could be removed from the complex by biothiol in the solution, which restored the fluorescence of CDC to achieve the “on” process. This process was selective for biothiols (especially for glutathione) among saccharides, dopamine and amino acids and the limit of detection was 2.02μM (S/N=3). Due to the dependence of the fluorescence restoration on the concentration of glutathione, Au(III)/CDC was applied as the fluorescence sensor for detection of glutathione in the solution and cellular cytosol. By referring to the fluorescence change in the solution, the intracellular glutathione with/without oxygen stress was evaluated. As compared with the commercial assay, our Au(III)/CDC based assay was simple, facile and low cost, which would be useful to measure intracellular glutathione at different cellular states.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Cluster
Au(III)
Glutathione
“Off–on” fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Au(III) and GSH
Evaluation of GSH in MCF-7 cells
Mechanism and characterization of the fluorescence “off–on” approach
Fluorescence “off” of CDs in the presence of Au(III)
Fluorescence restoration of Au(III)/CDC by GSH
Measurement of GSH in cellular cytosol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (a, c) and the size distribution histograms (b, d) of CDs and Au(III)/CDC. Inset: the HRTEM image of individual CDs. (a, b) CDs and (c, d) Au(III)/CDC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, Au(III)/CDC and Au(III)/CDC+GSH. (b) XRD patterns of Au(III)/CDC and Au(III)/CDC+GSH. (c) Effect of pH on I 0/I (Au(III)/CDC) and I'/I'0 (Au(III)/CDC+GSH). I 0 and I were the PL intensities (440nm) of CDs at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of 150μM Au(III), respectively. I'0 and I' were the PL intensities (440nm) of Au(III)/CDC at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of 300μM GSH, respectively. (d) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CDs, Au(III)/CDC and Au(III)/CDC+GSH. The gray line was the instrument response function.

(a) Selectivity of CDs with different positive ions at the same concentration (150μM). I 0 and I were the PL intensities (440nm) of CDs at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of different positive ions. (b) The relationship between (I 0/I) and concentration of Au(III) from 0 to 175μM (inset was a linear region). I 0 and I were the PL intensities (440nm) of CDs at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of Au(III), respectively.

(a) Response of Au(III)/CDC system to different biological molecules at the same concentration (300μM). I'0 and I' were the PL intensities (440nm) of Au(III)/CDC at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of different biological molecules, respectively. (b) The relationship between (I'/I'0) and concentration of GSH from 0 to 300μM (inset was a linear region) at pH=3.0. I'0 and I' were the PL intensities (440nm) of Au(III)/CDC at 360nm excitation in the absence and presence of GSH.
Concentration of GSH in MCF-7 cells based on UV and PL strategies.
A schematic illustration about formation of Au(III)/CDC and detection for GSH.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect Au(III) and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Coordination Chemistry, Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing National Laboratory of Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coordination Chemistry, Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing National Laboratory of Microstructures, Nanjing University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huaian High-Tech Research Institute of Nanjing University, Huaian 223005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huaian High-Tech Research Institute of Nanjing University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Coordination Chemistry, Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing National Laboratory of Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China. Fax: +86 25 83621337.
Corresponding author at: Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China. Fax: +86 25 83686197.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we reported for the first time that Au(III) decorated carbon dot cluster (Au(III)/CDC) was synthesized to detect glutathione through fluorescence “off–on” approach. The “off” process was realized by the introduction of Au(III) on luminescent carbon dots (CDs), which formed the complex of Au(III)/CDC and quenched the fluorescence of CDs efficiently. This “off” process was used to detect Au(III) with the selectivity among 21 metal ions and the limitation was 0.48μM (S/N=3). Au(III) could be removed from the complex by biothiol in the solution, which restored the fluorescence of CDC to achieve the “on” process. This process was selective for biothiols (especially for glutathione) among saccharides, dopamine and amino acids and the limit of detection was 2.02μM (S/N=3). Due to the dependence of the fluorescence restoration on the concentration of glutathione, Au(III)/CDC was applied as the fluorescence sensor for detection of glutathione in the solution and cellular cytosol. By referring to the fluorescence change in the solution, the intracellular glutathione with/without oxygen stress was evaluated. As compared with the commercial assay, our Au(III)/CDC based assay was simple, facile and low cost, which would be useful to measure intracellular glutathione at different cellular states.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) are widely applied in bio-imaging, biological labeling and sensing due to their excellent fluorescence, low-cost and eco-friendliness (Baker and Baker, 2010; Cao et al., 2012; Ding et al., 2014; Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Li et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2013, 2014; Sun et al., 2006; Wang and Qu, 2013; Zhang and Lin, 2012). Recently, the fluorescence of CDs and CNPS were observed to be turned off in the presence of metal ions, and restored after the formation of complexes between the metal ions and the chelators. Based on this mechanism, a lot of fluorescence “off–on” sensors developed from CDs or CNPs were designed for the detection of chelators, most of which were important in biology. For instance, photoluminescent CNPs were synthesized from dopamine (DA) and Fe3+ was added to quench the fluorescence, which was restored in the presence of DA. As a result, a simple “mix-and-detect” system was achieved for rapid detection of DA (Qu et al., 2013). The quenching of CDs fluorescence using Cu2+ was reported and the restoration of fluorescence was observed after the introduction of l-cysteine (l-Cys) (Zong et al., 2014). It was demonstrated that the “off” fluorescence of CDs could be achieved by using chromium (VI) and the “on” fluorescence was realized with ascorbic acid (Zheng et al., 2013b). Overall, a series of metal ions, including K+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr4+ had been reported to turn off the fluorescence of CDs (Dong et al., 2012; Guo et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2014; Zheng et al., 2013b; Zhou et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013a, 2013b; Zong et al., 2014). However, to the best of our knowledge, quenching of fluorescence of CDs by Au (III) has not been achieved. Au(III) detection is critically important since Au(III) in aqueous solution could cause toxicity to the living system through binding with certain enzymes or DNA (Choi et al., 2013; Kundu et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2013b; Yuan et al., 2011).

Meanwhile, since Au(III) has strong interaction with thiol functional group, the “off” fluorescence of CDs by Au(III) could be recovered by biothiols and served as an “off–on” fluorescent probe for the detection of biothiols. Biothiols are a family of biomolecules with thiol group, which are well known to play indispensable roles in cellular and biological processes (Hong et al., 2006; Xu and Hepel, 2011; Yang et al., 2013a). Glutathione (GSH) is the main biothiol in cells (90% thiol in the cells) that protects proteins and other cellular components from oxidation by reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Yin et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2012a; Yuan et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2013a). Hence, the analysis of biothiols in cells will offer the information about the amount of GSH. The change of intracellular GSH concentration is closely associated with the developmental state of physiological disorder such as cancer, AIDS and heart disease (Leung et al., 2013; Zhai et al., 2013). Though various analytical methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Reed et al., 1980), colorimetry (Durocher et al., 2009), electrochemistry (Safavi et al., 2009) and luminescence analysis (Deng et al. 2011) have been developed to determine GSH, a simple and low-cost strategy for the detection of GSH in biological samples is highly desirable. The success of “off–on” fluorescence sensor based on CDs is one of the effective paths to achieve the aim.

In this work, novel CDs were synthesized from honey, whose fluorescence was quenched by Au(III) and the Au(III) decorated carbon dot cluster (Au(III)/CDC) was then formed. After the introduction of GSH into Au(III)/CDC, the fluorescence of the system could be turned on by the complex of Au(III) and thiols. This platform was simple, low cost and selective, which was applied to detect GSH in MCF-7 cells with/without the oxygen stress.

Black forest honey (83.73wt%) was purchased from Langnese (Germany). Ammonia solution (25–28wt%) and hydrochloric acid (36–38wt%) were obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA, 98%), glucose (AR) and reduced l-glutathione (GSH, 98%) were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). d-aspartic acid (Asp, 99%), l-cysteine (Cys, 99%) and l-glutamic Acid (Glu, 99%) were purchased from Adamas-beta Inc. (Shanghai, China). α-Lactose (Lac), sucrose, β-alanine (Ala), l-arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly) and l-lysine (Lys) with analytical reagent grade were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (China) and used without further purification. l-asparagine (Asn), l-glutamine (Gln), l-histidine (Hls), l-leucine (Leu), l-isoleucine (lle), l-methionine (Met), l-phenylalanine (Phe), l-proline (Pro), l-serine (Ser), l-threonine (Thr), l-tryptophan (Trp), l-tyrosine (Tyr) and l-valine (Val) with analytical reagent grade were brought BioDuly Ltd. (Nanjing, China). 5, 5′-Dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Deionized water (18.2MΩcm−1) obtained from the Millipore system was used throughout all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired with a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) result was obtained by a Brookhaven BI-200SM. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) with Cu Ka radiation (40kV, 40mA, λ=1.54051Å). Elemental analysis was measured with a CHN–O-rapid elemental analyzer (Heraeus, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was measured by a Vector-22 spectrometer (Bruker) in the range from 400 to 4000cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by a PHI 5000 VersaProbe XPS instrument (UlVAC-PHI, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was carried on a UV-1800(PC) UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mapada, China). All fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Scientific, Japan) with slit width of 5nm for both excitation and emission. The time-resolved fluorescence decay was obtained with a TimePro-01 lifetime fluorescence spectroscopy (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France) with a 371nm laser as the excitation source.

1g of black forest honey was diluted with 18mL of deionized water and 2mL of ammonia solution, which was stirred for 5min. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven at 200°C for 12h with heating rate at 5°Cmin−1. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged with 10,000rpm for 3min to precipitate out particle aggregates. The upper clear solution was dialyzed for 2 days (MWCO =3.5kD, renew water every 12h) and the dialysate outside the dialysis membrane was collected and evaporated to 20mL. The brown solution was washed by trichloromethane for 3 times to remove impurity. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the pure CDs with the yield of 237mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Au(III) and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Au(III) and GSH was performed in HCl solution (pH=3.00) at room temperature. In a typical experiment, CDs solution and Au(III) solution were added into HCl solution and the final concentration of Au(III) was in the range of 0–150μM. The emission spectra were recorded after 30min at 360nm excitation. For comparison, other positive ions were added in the same way at the final concentration of 150μM. To detect GSH, GSH solution (pH=3.00) was added into Au(III)/CDC solution for 1min before recording the emission spectra at 360nm excitation. The final concentration of GSH was in the range of 0–300μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of GSH in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 cells were chosen as the real sample for analysis. The cells were cultured in 75cm2 flasks with Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing high glucose at 37°C in 5% CO2. After trypsinization, the cells were collected and washed by PBS for 3 times. 0.5mL of the cells were lysed by addition of 4.5mL of HCl (pH=3.00) and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 5min.

For our photoluminescence (PL) strategy, 1mL of the cell lysate was added to 3mL Au(III)/CDC solution and the PL spectrum was measured at 360nm excitation. For comparison, UV method was employed according to the previous report (Dinçer and Zihnioğlu, 2010). The mixture was comprised of 0.5mL of the cell lysate, 3.3mL of Tris–HCl buffer (0.1MpH 8.2) and 0.2mL Ellmann reagent (60mg DTNB /100mL Tris–HCl buffer). The UV–vis absorbance of the mixture was recorded at 412nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was obtained by the following equation:
Q
Y
s a m
= Q
Y
r e f
I
s a m
A
r e f
n
s a m
2
I
r e f
A
s a m
n
r e f
2
where “sam” and “ref” refer to sample and reference, respectively. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY=0.54 at 360nm) was chosen as the reference. “I” is the integrated emission intensity, which could be calculated from the emission spectra at 360nm excitation. “A” represents UV–vis absorbance at 360nm, which is controlled under 0.1 in the 10mm quartz absorbance cell to avoid re-absorption effects and “n” is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both quinine sulfate and CDs.

The original curve of time-resolved fluorescence decay was fitted as below (Tang et al., 2013):
I ( t ) = A +
B 1
exp ( − t /
τ 1
) +
B 2
exp ( − t /
τ 2
)
where B 1 and B 2 are the relative amplitudes and τ 1 and τ 2 are the component lifetimes, respectively. Average fluorescence lifetime 〈τ〉 is calculated according to the following equation:
< τ > =
B 1
B 1
+
B 2
τ 1
+
B 2
B 1
+
B 2
τ 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and characterization of the fluorescence “off–on” approach
ce:section.ce:para: This strategy of fluorescence “off” and “on” was summarized in Scheme 1. As the base material of fluorescent probes, CDs demonstrated strong blue photoluminescence under 365nm excitation. The high fluorescent CDs were quenched by adding Au(III), which formed Au(III) decorated carbon dot cluster (Au(III)/CDC). This complex was broken in presence of GSH via strong interaction between Au(III) and thiols, resulting in the restoration of the fluorescence.

We employed honey as the raw material to synthesize CDs by one step hydrothermal treatment in presence of ammonia. The obtained CDs were dispersed well in water and could be stable for several months.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology and size of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, the CDs were nearly spherical and well separated from each other with the size smaller than 8nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1a) of one nanoparticle revealed that CDs had a lattice spacing of 0.23nm, which was corresponded to the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon as reported (Lu et al., 2009). The size distribution histogram of particles measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) in Fig. 1b confirmed that the diameters of CDs had a range of 3–8nm with the maximum of 4.41nm, which agreed with TEM results. Fig. 1c showed that Au(III)/CDC appeared as a cluster morphology of several CDs due to the addition of Au(III). The size distribution histograms in Fig. 1d demonstrated the Au(III)/CDC had a size from 35 to 50nm with the maximum of 40nm, which confirmed the increased size from CDs to Au(III)/CDC. The large size of Au(III)/CDC was attributed to the aggregation of carbon dots (Lai et al., 2013). The carbon dots recovered to less than 10nm size after the loss of Au(III), which was confirmed by TEM imaging (Fig. S1).

Elemental analysis was measured to analyze the change of elemental composition from honey to CDs. As shown in Table S1, the significant increase of nitrogen content were presented by comparing CDs and reactant, which confirmed that the abundant of nitrogen was doped by hydrothermal treatment in presence of ammonia. Moreover, the carbon content increased after hydrothermal process for CDs, which was caused by dehydration. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterize the surface element composition and states of the resultant CDs. The XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. S2a) showed three peaks centered at 283.3eV (C1s), 398.8eV (N1s) and 530.4eV (O1s). These peaks suggested that nitrogen was doped on the surface of CDs and was corresponding to elemental analysis results. Furthermore, the four peaks at 284.4, 285.0, 285.8 and 287.7eV in the C1s spectra (Fig. S2b) were assigned to C=C, C–OH, C–N and C=O groups, respectively. N1s spectra in Fig. S2c showed two peaks at 399.1eV and 400.5eV, which was attributed to C–N–C and N–H, respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. S2d) exhibited two peaks at 530.8 and 532.0eV, which were attributed to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively. The functional groups of CDs were further identified using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S3. The peaks at 3388 and 1077cm−1 were attributed to O–H stretching vibration. Absorption peaks at 3166cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of N–H formed under hydrothermal treatment in ammonia water condition. The new peak at around 1590cm−1 was contributed to the vibration band of C=C, which was formed by hydrothermal treatment.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra. The UV–vis absorption of the as-prepared CDs in Fig. S4a showed a strong peak at about 274nm, which was assigned to the typical characteristic of CDs (Li et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2012b). The photoluminescence emission spectra of CDs with various excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. S4b. When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 420nm, the wavelength of the maximum emission shifted from 414 to 475nm. This redshift of maximum emission wavelength was similar to other reported carbon dots (Li et al., 2013; Liang et al., 2013; Zhang and Chen, 2014; Zhu et al., 2013b). The photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) of CDs was 7.64% with quinine sulfate as a standard when excited at 360nm (Table S2).

The fluorescence spectra under 360nm excitation of CDs, Au(III)/CDC and Au(III)/CDC+GSH were demonstrated in Fig. 2a. To quench the fluorescence of CDs, 150μM Au(III) was introduced to the CDs with the concentration of 0.1mg/mL. The fluorescence of CDs was significantly quenched in the presence of Au(III). Due to the strong interaction between Au(III) and thiol group, it was reasonable to propose that the addition of thiol would break the complex of Au(III)/CDC to restore the fluorescence of CDs. Experimentally, to realized the fluorescence “on” process, GSH with thiol group was added to the Au(III)/CDC and the fluorescence of the system was turned on after the addition of GSH into Au(III)/CDC.

To elucidate the valence state of Au element in Au(III)/CDC and Au(III)/CDC+GSH, XRD was measured and showed in Fig. 2b. A clear Au(III) peak was observed at 32.5° for Au(III)/CDC, which indicated that Au(III) was not reduced in Au(III)/CDC. The XRD pattern of Au(III)/CDC+GSH also demonstrated the existence of Au(III) in the presence of GSH.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence “off–on” process was investigated and demonstrated in Fig. 2c. The PL intensities at 440nm under 360nm excitation of initial CDs and Au(III)/CDC were named I 0 and I, respectively. A peak value of I 0/I was observed at pH of 3.0. The largest ratio of I' and I'0 was also at pH 3.0, in which I' and I'0 represented the PL intensities at 440nm under 360nm excitation of Au(III)/CDC in the presence and absence of GSH. Thus the optimal detection pH for the whole experiments was chosen as 3.0.

To investigate the fluorescence “off–on” mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using the lifetime fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence lifetimes were calculated in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2d, CDs had a lifetime of 4.61ns, while the lifetime of Au(III)/CDC decreased to 3.86ns. The shorter lifetime revealed a dynamic quenching mechanism after the introduction of Au(III) on CDs (Liu et al. 2013). The lifetime of Au(III)/CDC+GSH returned to 4.32ns, which indicated the dissociation of Au(III) from the surface of carbon dot cluster due to the stronger interaction between Au(III) and GSH.

The time-dependent PL intensity of Au(III)/CDC was shown in Fig. S5a, which indicated that fluorescence intensity decreased quickly after addition of Au(III) to CDs and was stable after 25min. Meanwhile, the time-dependent PL intensity of Au(III)/CDC+GSH was shown in Fig. S5b, which indicated that fluorescence was restored to the maximum value in 1min. After 1min, the fluorescence intensity was stabilized, indicating a fast recovery of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “off” of CDs in the presence of Au(III)
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the specificity of fluorescence quenching by Au(III), the fluorescence emissions of CDs with different metal ions were measured at 360nm excitation. Fig. 3a showed that CDs with Au(III) system had the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency, as compared with CDs with other ions including Ag+, K+, Li+, Na+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ce3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+. The quenching efficiency of CDs with Au(III) was as high as 83.3% and the corresponding I 0/I was 5.99, which supported that the CDs had a selective response to Au(III). The most likely reason for this was that Au(III) had higher thermodynamic affinity with N and O of the CDs than other metal ions, which caused the aggregation of the CDs to quench the fluorescence. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report about the quenching of CDs fluorescence by Au(III) selectively. Fig. S6 showed the fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Au(III) (0–175μM) at 360nm excitation. The PL intensity at 440nm decreased gradually as the concentration of Au(III) increased from 0 to 175μM. The relationship between I 0/I (the ratio of PL intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Au(III)) and c (the concentration of Au(III)) was presented in Fig. 3b. A good fitted regression line (R 2=0.992) was shown as below:
I 0
I
= 0.0226 c + 0.990
in which c was in the range of 0–75μM. The good fitted regression line was close to the Stern–Volmer equation. The detection limit was 0.48μM (S/N=3), which was comparable with the recent report (Kundu et al., 2012). These results indicated that the CDs could be used to measure Au(III) in the solution quantitatively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence restoration of Au(III)/CDC by GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the mechanism of restoration by the interaction of Au(III) and thiol, different biological molecules with the same concentration (300μM) including glucose, sacrose, lactose, dopamine (DA), alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), asparagine (Asn), aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys), glutamine (Gln), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), histidine (Hls), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (lle), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), valine (Val) and GSH were added into Au(III)/CDC system, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360nm excitation. As shown in Fig. 4a, saccharides and non-thiol amino acids did not cause significant change of fluorescence intensities. Since Cys and Met had sulfur group, they could partially restore the fluorescence of Au(III)/CDC, but their fluorescence signals were lower than that of Au(III)/CDC in the presence of GSH. The 4.84±0.06 fold enhanced fluorescence of our probe after reacted with GSH was comparative with the recently reported “off–on” fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and metal ion, which was shown in Table S4. In addition, the enhancement was dependent on the amount of Au(III). As shown in Fig. S7, when we increased the concentration of Au(III) from 150μM to 300μM, the fluorescence was approx. 10-fold enhanced after the reaction of the probe with 500μM GSH. The linearity range was from 0 to 300μM with the detection limit of 3.65μM (S/N=3). The possible reason for the selectivity for GSH over Cys was that GSH had the multidentate anchor and the specific steric structure, which led to the coordination priority with gold atom (Shi et al., 2014). The results indicated that the developed sensing system exhibited excellent selectivity of GSH. To study the competitive reaction between GSH and amino acids, 150μM amino acids were added into Au(III)/CDC with 300μM GSH and the result was shown in Fig. S8. No more fluorescence was observed after the introduction of amino acids, which indicated that amino acids had no competitive reaction with GSH.

Due to the high specificity of GSH on the fluorescence restoration, Au(III)/CDC could be used as the fluorescence probe for the detection of GSH. Different concentrations of GSH (0–300μM) were added into the Au(III)/CDC to recover the fluorescence at pH=3.0. As shown in Fig. S9, the intensity of fluorescence increased with the increase of GSH concentration, which indicated the increased dissociation of Au(III) from the surface of carbon dot cluster. The relationship between I'/I'0 (the ratio of PL intensities of Au(III)/CDC in the presence and absence of GSH) and c' (the concentration of GSH) was shown in Fig. 4b. A good fitted regression line (R 2=0.999) was also presented as below:
I `
I
` 0
= 0.0117 c ` + 0.982
in which c' was in the range of 0–150μM, and the detection limit was 2.02μM (S/N=3).

To confirm our method was potentially useful for biosamples, we also test our assay at pH=7.0. As shown in Fig. S10, the value of I'/I'0 (the ratio of PL intensities of Au(III)/CDC in the presence and absence of GSH) increased with more GSH and kept constant when the concentration was higher than 50μM. The fitted regression line (R 2=0.955) was shown as below:
I `
I
` 0
= 0.0112 c ` + 1.0049
in which c' was in the range of 0–10μM, and the detection limit was 1.86μM (S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of GSH in cellular cytosol
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing system of Au(III)/CDC was used to evaluate GSH amount in cells to test its analytical ability for practical application. The cultured 106 MCF-7 cells were lysed in HCl (pH=3.0) and measured by our PL strategy and UV spectroscopy, respectively. By referring to the standard curve in Fig. 4b, the concentration of GSH in MCF-7 cells was calculated to be 5.589±0.378mM by PL method, which was exhibited in Fig. 5. The results were agreed with the normal level reported before (0.5–10mM) (Zheng et al., 2013a). Although this method could not distinguish GSH and the other biothiols, it could give the information about intracellular GSH because over 90% biothiol was GSH.

Since oxygen stress had been known to oxidize GSH into the oxidative state, the cells were treated with 0.5mM H2O2 for 12h so that the concentration of reductive state of GSH decreased. Using our PL strategy, the GSH amount in cells cultured with 0.5mM H2O2 was 1.653±0.072mM. The correlation of our result with alteration of intracellular GSH concentration confirmed that Au(III)/CDC could be competent to estimate GSH level in cells. To further confirm the accuracy of our measurement, the well-developed UV strategy was applied to measure intracellular GSH. By referring to the standard curve in Fig. S11, the GSH values in the cells without/with oxygen stress were 5.651±0.245 and 1.817±0.058mM, respectively, which were close to the values collected by the PL strategy. Although 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) had been applied as the commercial UV probe, the easy oxidation and high toxicity limited the assay for the in vivo study. Therefore, developing novel low toxicity nanoparticles was needed for the in-vivo measurement. In our work, carbon dots based complex had low cytotoxicity and could be applied for the future in-vivo measurement. Also, our materials in the solution had high stability at room temperature for 3 months, which facilitated the application.

In summary, the Au(III)/CDC was synthesized as an efficient “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect GSH for the first time. Au(III) could quench the fluorescence of CDs by the formation of Au(III)/CDC complex. Upon the addition of GSH into Au(III)/CDC, the fluorescence was recovered, which was attributed to the dissociation of Au(III) from the surface of CDC by the stronger interaction between Au(III) and GSH. Additionally, this sensing platform exhibited high selectivity and efficiency toward Au(III) in solution and GSH in cellular cytosol. This efficient strategy was simple, facile and low cost, which had potential applications for developing novel biological and environmental sensors. The future work will aim at the application of this novel materials inside the living cells so that the intracellular GSH could be tracked in real time.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21104032), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK2011413), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 1104020505) and the Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province (2014, No. KYLX_0030). The authors gratefully acknowledge Dr. Dechen Jiang for design of the analytical experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.12.027.
